title: Measuring the Mess
subtitle: Risk registers, RCSA, KRIs and loss data

hook.metaphor: A good head chef doesn't just *feel* that things are getting risky — they track it. How many dishes came back tonight? How close did we get to running out of gas? Operational risk teams do the same: they turn the vague dread of 'something could go wrong' into things you can list, score, and watch. You can't manage what you don't measure.
hook.scenario: Two teams both 'feel fine' about their risks. One keeps a register, scores each risk by likelihood and impact, and watches early-warning indicators. The other just hopes. Six months later, only one of them saw the problem coming. Which — and why?

learn: Step one is the **risk register**: a living list of what could go wrong, each scored by — you guessed it — **likelihood × impact** (the same idea from Credit Risk Level 1, and the same colours as a heat map). It forces the vague into the specific.

Step two is the **RCSA — Risk and Control Self-Assessment.** Teams systematically ask, for each risk, 'how bad could this be, and how good are our controls against it?' It surfaces the gaps *before* they cost money. Boring, structured, and quietly powerful.

Step three is watching **leading vs lagging indicators.** **Loss event data** (what already went wrong, and what it cost) is *lagging* — it tells you about the past. **Key Risk Indicators (KRIs)** are *leading* — early-warning metrics like staff turnover, system downtime, or rising error rates that flash amber *before* the loss lands. The team that 'saw it coming' was watching its KRIs.

Be honest about the limits, though. Operational risk is hard to quantify precisely — rare, severe events don't fit neat statistics, and **model risk** means your measurement can itself be wrong. So good teams pair the numbers with judgement and scenario thinking ('what's our worst plausible day?') rather than trusting a single tidy figure.
bridge: So at work, you can push any team beyond 'we feel okay' with three questions: *Where's your risk register? Have you self-assessed your controls (RCSA)? What early-warning indicators are you actually watching?* A risk that's been named, scored, and monitored is a risk you can get ahead of; an unmeasured one just waits to surprise you.

interactions.title: The measurement toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each tool to what it does.

pairs.right: A living list of risks, each scored by likelihood × impact
Teams self-assess how strong their controls are
A record of what went wrong and what it cost (lagging)
An early-warning metric that flags trouble ahead (leading)
Imagining your worst plausible day to test readiness

interactions.title: Leading or lagging?
interactions.prompt: Sort each metric. Leading indicators warn you *before*; lagging ones tell you *after*.

items.label: Number of losses recorded last quarter
Rising system error rate this week
Spiking staff turnover in a key team
Total fraud losses booked last year
Growing backlog of overdue control checks

interactions.title: Trust the single number?
interactions.prompt: A vendor sells a model that outputs one precise figure: 'your operational risk = ₹4.2 crore.' How should you treat it?

options.label: Accept it — it's a precise, scientific number.
options.feedback: False precision. Rare, severe events resist tidy statistics, and the model itself carries model risk. One exact figure hides huge uncertainty.

options.label: Use it as one input, alongside judgement and scenarios.
options.feedback: Right. Numbers inform but don't replace thinking. Pair the estimate with 'what's our worst plausible day?' and expert judgement.

options.label: Ignore all measurement — it's hopeless.
options.feedback: Too far the other way. Imperfect measurement still beats hoping. Register it, score it, watch KRIs — just don't worship a single number.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “You measure operational risk by listing and scoring it (register), checking your controls (RCSA), and watching early-warning signs (KRIs) — while staying humble that no single number is the whole truth.”

checkpoint.q: A Key Risk Indicator (KRI) is valuable because it…
options: Gives early warning *before* a loss lands
Replaces the need for any controls
checkpoint.explain: KRIs are leading indicators — rising error rates or turnover flash amber before the loss arrives.

checkpoint.q: An RCSA (Risk and Control Self-Assessment) is mainly about…
options: Teams assessing how strong their controls are against each risk
checkpoint.explain: It's a structured self-check of risks and the controls meant to contain them, surfacing gaps early.

checkpoint.q: Why be cautious about a single precise operational-risk number?
options: Rare severe events resist neat statistics and the model itself can be wrong
checkpoint.explain: False precision hides real uncertainty; pair estimates with judgement and scenario thinking.
